questionText: $ABCD$ is a quadrilateral. Consider the statements $P:\ ABCD$ is a rhombus, and $Q:\ ABCD$ has perpendicular diagonals. Determine the logical relationship between $P$ and $Q$, deciding which of $\Rightarrow$, $\Leftarrow$ or $\Leftrightarrow$ applies. Hence state whether being a rhombus is a necessary condition, a sufficient condition, both, or neither, for having perpendicular diagonals.

steps.workingLatex: P:\ ABCD \text{ is a rhombus}, \qquad Q:\ \text{diagonals of } ABCD \text{ are perpendicular}
steps.explanation: To pick the right connective we must test whether each property forces the other. Naming the statements keeps the two directions apart.

steps.description: Recall the defining property of a rhombus.
steps.explanation: A rhombus is a parallelogram whose four sides are equal in length. This high degree of symmetry is what constrains its diagonals.

steps.description: Test the forward direction P implies Q.
steps.workingLatex: \text{diagonals bisect each other and are perpendicular bisectors}
steps.explanation: In a rhombus the diagonals are perpendicular bisectors of each other: each diagonal splits it into two congruent triangles that meet at right angles at the centre. So a rhombus always has perpendicular diagonals.
Every rhombus automatically has perpendicular diagonals, so the property $Q$ is guaranteed by being a rhombus.

steps.description: Set up a test of the converse.
steps.workingLatex: \text{seek } ABCD \text{ with perpendicular diagonals but not a rhombus}
steps.explanation: To decide the converse we look for a single quadrilateral that has perpendicular diagonals yet fails to be a rhombus. If even one exists, the converse is false.

steps.description: Choose a kite as the counterexample.
steps.explanation: A kite has two pairs of adjacent equal sides but not all four equal, so it is not a rhombus. It is the natural shape to try because its lopsided symmetry still constrains the diagonals.

steps.description: Show the kite has perpendicular diagonals.
steps.explanation: The axis of a kite is the perpendicular bisector of the other diagonal, so the diagonals meet at right angles. Thus $Q$ holds while $P$ fails, giving a valid counterexample.
Because the kite satisfies $Q$ but not $P$, the converse fails, which immediately rules out the two-way symbol $\Leftrightarrow$.
Only the forward implication survives, so the correct connective is the one-way arrow from rhombus to perpendicular diagonals.

steps.workingLatex: P \text{ sufficient for } Q, \ \text{ not necessary}
steps.explanation: Being a rhombus guarantees perpendicular diagonals, so it is sufficient; but perpendicular diagonals can occur without a rhombus (the kite), so it is not necessary.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $P \Rightarrow Q$ only. A rhombus always has perpendicular diagonals, but a kite has perpendicular diagonals without being a rhombus, so the converse is false. Being a rhombus is therefore a sufficient but not a necessary condition for having perpendicular diagonals.
